beats.narration: Claude researches the discriminant — the formula hidden inside the quadratic formula that tells you everything about a parabola's roots before you solve it.
beats.narration_text: This is Liam, in for Bear.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: This is Liam, in for Bear.

beats.narration: The discriminant b squared minus four a c appears in every quadratic formula. But where did it come from, and why does its sign reveal the nature of the roots?
We ask Claude to research the history of the discriminant formula from ancient algebra through its modern formulation.

props.command:
claude "research the history of the discriminant formula
  from ancient Babylonian algebra
  to al-Khwarizmi and Arabic algebra
  to modern quadratic formula notation"

beats.narration: Claude traces the path: Babylonian tablets solved quadratics by geometric completion. Al-Khwarizmi formalized it in 820 CE. The modern b squared minus four a c notation came in the nineteenth century.

props.code:
# discriminant_history.md — Claude research

~1800 BCE  Babylon: geometric completion
            of squares on clay tablets

  820 CE   al-Khwarizmi: Al-Kitab al-mukhtasar
            first systematic solution of ax^2+bx+c=0

 1637 CE   Descartes: analytic geometry;
            roots become x-intercepts

 1800s     Modern notation b^2-4ac formalized
            discriminant = 'that which discriminates'

beats.narration: Three cases: discriminant positive means two real roots and the parabola crosses twice. Zero means one repeated root — it just touches. Negative means no real roots — the parabola misses the x-axis entirely.
Now Claude explains why the sign of b squared minus four a c determines the nature of the roots algebraically.

props.command:
claude "explain why b^2-4ac determines root type:
  positive: two real roots
  zero: one repeated root
  negative: complex roots"

beats.narration: The quadratic formula takes a square root of b squared minus four a c. A positive under the radical gives two real solutions. Zero gives one. A negative makes the square root imaginary — complex roots, no real crossing.
The discriminant is the most efficient diagnostic in algebra: one number reveals whether a quadratic has two real roots, a repeated root, or no real roots.
Next: the unit circle — connecting angles to trigonometric values with Claude Code.
